home.hero_title: Compare EV Charging Prices Across Every Network
home.hero_subtitle: Find the cheapest charging for your electric vehicle — by network, state, and vehicle type.
home.faq_q1: What is the cheapest EV charging network?
home.faq_a1: Tesla Supercharger offers competitive rates at $0.25/kWh for non-Tesla vehicles. Volta offers free Level 2 charging at select locations. ChargePoint and Flo often have the most competitive per-kWh pricing with membership.
home.faq_a2: DC Fast Charging (DCFC) delivers power directly as DC electricity to your battery, bypassing the vehicle's onboard charger. This enables speeds of 50-350kW, compared to 3-22kW for Level 2 AC charging.
home.faq_q3: What is NACS vs CCS connector?
home.faq_a3: NACS (North American Charging Standard) is Tesla's connector now adopted by most automakers. CCS (Combined Charging System) is the previous standard used by many EVs. Adapters are available for both.
home.faq_q4: How much does it cost to charge an EV from 10% to 80%?
home.faq_a4: Costs vary by vehicle battery size and network pricing. A Tesla Model 3 (82 kWh battery) costs roughly $14–$28 for a 10–80% charge depending on the network. Use our calculator for exact figures.
home.faq_q5: Which EV network has the most stations?
home.faq_a5: ChargePoint has the largest network with over 25,000 charging locations across the US. Tesla Supercharger leads in fast-charging reliability with 2,200+ dedicated DC fast charge stations.
